mission.title: Install and Configure Dex on Kubernetes
mission.description: Dex is an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity and OAuth 2.0 provider that allows applications to authenticate users through various identity providers. Installing Dex provides a centralized authentication service that can integrate with existing identity systems, enhancing security and user management.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Dex Helm chart repository to your Helm client.
```bash
helm repo add dex https://charts.dexidp.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Dex using Helm
steps.description:
Install Dex with the specified version and create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install dex dex/dex --version v2.45.0 --namespace dex --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Dex pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n dex
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access Dex
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Dex service locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/dex 5556:5556 -n dex
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for Dex by editing the deployment.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment dex -n dex
# Add the following under spec.template.spec.containers.resources:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: 500m
#     memory: 512Mi
#   requests:
#     cpu: 250m
#     memory: 256Mi
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Dex Helm release from the cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall dex --namespace dex
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any custom resources associated with Dex.
```bash
kubectl delete crd authrequests.dex.idp.io
kubectl delete crd connectors.dex.idp.io
kubectl delete crd oauth2clients.dex.idp.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace created for Dex.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace dex
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Backup the current configuration and state of Dex.
```bash
kubectl get all -n dex -o yaml > dex-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to fetch the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Dex
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the Dex installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade dex dex/dex --version v2.45.0 --namespace dex
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Dex pods to ensure they are running the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n dex
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Dex pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n dex
# Look for any configuration errors or missing dependencies.
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Dex service, ensure the service is running and the port-forwarding is set up correctly.
```bash
kubectl get svc -n dex
# Check if the service is listed and then re-run the port-forward command.
```

troubleshooting.title: Authentication failures
troubleshooting.description:
If authentication fails, verify the connectors and configuration in the Dex deployment.
```bash
kubectl get configmap -n dex
# Check the configuration for any misconfigurations in connectors.
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Dex will have all pods running in the 'dex' namespace, and you will be able to access the Dex service via port 5556. You should also be able to authenticate users through the configured identity providers.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add dex https://charts.dexidp.io
helm repo update
helm install dex dex/dex --version v2.45.0 --namespace dex --create-namespace

kubectl edit deployment dex -n dex
# Add the following under spec.template.spec.containers.resources:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: 500m
#     memory: 512Mi
#   requests:
#     cpu: 250m
#     memory: 256Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed on your local machine.
